source.title: Somatic and vicarious pain are represented by dissociable multivariate brain patterns

confidence_rationale: Strong multi-study design (primary study plus two replications, out-of-sample generalisation, cross-submodality validation). For ARC-058 the evidence is genuinely mixed: the somatic and vicarious pain patterns are dissociable at 100% out-of-sample, which weakens a strong shared-trunk interpretation; but the somatic pattern generalises across thermal, mechanical, and electrical pain, which supports a within-somatic-modality shared trunk (the most directly relevant case for ARC-058's z_harm_s). Confidence weighted by the indirect mapping -- somatic vs vicarious is a larger architectural gap than z_harm_s vs z_harm_a.
failure_signatures: A strong shared-trunk model with no per-stream heads predicts somatic and vicarious pain signatures should be highly overlapping; Krishnan 2016 shows they are fully dissociable at chance cross-prediction, favouring per-stream heads (ARC-058 heads OR full ARC-033 independence).

A fully partitioned model with no shared substrate across somatic pain sub-modalities predicts the NPS should not generalise from thermal to mechanical or electrical pain; Krishnan 2016 shows it does, which supports a shared trunk at least WITHIN the somatic-pain cluster (the case ARC-058 actually targets for z_harm_s).

mapping.source_claim_statement: Multivariate brain patterns for somatic and vicarious pain are fully dissociable (100% out-of-sample classification, each pattern at chance in predicting the other). The somatic pain pattern (originally developed on thermal pain) generalises to mechanical and electrical pain, supporting a shared somatic-pain representation across noxious sub-modalities. The somatic/vicarious dissociation was replicated in a second study.
mapping.ree_translation: Two architectural questions for ARC-058 in one paper. (a) At the larger modality gap (somatic vs vicarious), full dissociation supports stream-specific heads and weakens a strong shared-trunk reading. (b) At the within-somatic-pain scale (thermal/mechanical/electrical), the NPS generalises -- this is exactly the case ARC-058 cares about: the z_harm_s stream should share substrate across nociceptive sub-modalities, and it does. For z_harm_s alone, ARC-058's trunk architecture is strongly supported. For the z_harm_s vs z_harm_a distinction, the somatic/vicarious evidence by analogy suggests heads dominate the decodable signal. Net direction: mixed, leaning supports the two-layer architecture.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Somatic vs vicarious is an empathy-for-pain contrast, not the affective-vs-sensory within-pain contrast ARC-058 actually posits. Transfer from 'observing a stranger in pain' to 'C-fibre-driven affective pain stream' is substantial -- vicarious pain patterns load on mentalising regions (TPJ, STS) that are unrelated to z_harm_a as REE conceives it. The within-somatic-pain generalisation is the more directly relevant evidence, and it is clean.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, three studies. Study 1: somatic thermal pain + vicarious pain (pictures). Study 2: generalisation of the somatic pattern to mechanical and electrical pain. Study 3: replication of the somatic/vicarious dissociation. Multivariate pattern analysis with out-of-sample cross-validation.

confidence_components.notes: Good source quality (eLife, three-study design, cross-submodality validation). Mapping fidelity is moderate -- the within-somatic generalisation is directly relevant to ARC-058's z_harm_s trunk claim; the somatic-vicarious dissociation is a weaker analogue of the z_harm_s vs z_harm_a question. Transfer risk is higher than for the Wager/Woo/Horing papers because 'vicarious' in Krishnan means observing someone else in pain, not an internal affective pain stream.
